question: Compare the 100% power flow conditions with minimum design flow conditions from Table 5.1-2.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems and the PZR is integrated with the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to more detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance by establishing steady-state parameters—such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures—across different power levels using thermal hydraulic modeling and verification. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions. These design bases collectively support the safety and performance of each NuScale Power Module.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation between primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures for maintaining reactor vessel integrity in the NuScale Power Plant. It details the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation over a 60-year design life, and confirms that all criteria remain within regulatory limits.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC regulations and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified according to specific regulatory guides and federal regulations, ensuring all conditions and requirements are met. Table 5.2-1 consolidates the specific ASME code cases used.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and it incorporates features for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining the integrity of the pressure boundary to prevent radionuclide release. The system interfaces with several other subsystems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control systems.

edges.description: Section B discusses the establishment of steady state values for system parameters at various power levels, which directly relates to the 100% power flow and minimum design flow conditions referenced in Table 5.1-2, while Section A describes system component operations that are influenced by these flow conditions.

Section A describes specific operational procedures involving system components like the pressurizer and chemical volume control system, which are part of the broader reactor coolant system design and functions outlined in Section B.

Section A discusses how steady state flow parameters are established for different power levels to optimize system performance, while Section B outlines the design basis and natural circulation flow approach of the reactor coolant system, providing the foundational context for the flow conditions compared in Table 5.1-2.

Section A outlines the design and components of the reactor coolant system, which establishes the operational parameters (such as flow conditions) that are then referenced in Section B's description of system operations and transitions, including flow adjustments during specific procedures.
Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at various power levels, including 100% and minimum design flow, which are implemented in the component and subsystem design described in Section A.
Section A provides detailed descriptions of the reactor coolant system components and subsystems that are summarized in Section B's general overview of the system's design basis and function.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity under various power conditions, while Section B describes system component operations during transitions, both of which are connected by the need to maintain safe pressure and temperature limits during different flow conditions as referenced in Table 5.1-2.

Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at various power levels, which Section A relies on to develop operating procedures that ensure reactor vessel integrity under both 100% power and minimum design flow conditions.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel integrity by not exceeding pressure-temperature limits during various power conditions, while Section B provides the design basis and description of the reactor coolant system that establishes the flow conditions (such as 100% power and minimum design flow) referenced in those procedures.

Section A outlines regulatory compliance and code requirements for overpressure protection, while Section B describes operational procedures affecting system component conditions, both of which are connected through the design and operational parameters (such as flow conditions) that must comply with the specified codes and requirements.

Section B establishes steady state flow parameters at various power levels, including 100% and minimum design flow, which Section A relies on to ensure overpressure protection and compliance with regulatory codes under those conditions.

Section A references compliance with regulatory codes for overpressure protection, while Section B provides the general design basis and description of the reactor coolant system, establishing the system context in which overpressure protection requirements are applied.

Section A discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure reactor vessel pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during various flow conditions, while Section B outlines compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection requirements, both addressing how the reactor vessel's integrity is maintained under different operational scenarios such as 100% power and minimum design flow.

Section A describes the function of the RCS degasification line and RVVs in removing noncondensible gases from the PZR during both normal and emergency operations, while Section B provides specific operational steps involving the degasification line and RVVs during system layup and pressure equalization, thus Section B specifies the operational context for the general system functions described in Section A.

Section A describes the system features and operational modes (including flow paths and components) that enable different flow conditions, while Section B evaluates how steady state flow parameters (such as those at 100% power and minimum design flow) are established and optimized based on the system's capabilities described in Section A.

Section A describes specific operational features of the RCS, such as degasification and emergency venting, while Section B provides the general design basis for the RCS, including its reliance on natural circulation, thus Section A provides details that specify the general system described in Section B.
